title: Joint equidistribution of arithmetic orbits, joinings, and rigidity of higher rank diagonalizable actions I
speaker: Elon Lindenstrauss
abstract: An important theme in homogenous dynamics is that two parameter diagonalizable actions have much more rigidity than one parameter actions. One manifestation of this rigidity is rigidity of joinings of such actions. Joinings are an important concept in the study of dynamical systems, and allow detection of "hidden common denominator" of two (or more) dynamical systems. While one parameter diagonalizable actions on homogenous spaces have lots of joinings, for two parameter actions any measurable joining can be provided to be in fact of a very precise algebraic kind.

proof_stages.label: Adelic arithmetic-orbit framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up adelic homogeneous spaces G(A)/G(Q), subgroups H defined over Q, normalized H(A)-invariant measures on shifted complete arithmetic orbits, finite-index near-invariance, and a volume notion for sequences going to infinity.

proof_stages.label: Individual equidistribution of torus orbits
proof_stages.summary: Specializing to anisotropic tori, especially the PGL2 case of packets of closed diagonal/geodesic orbits, the talk reviews Duke-type equidistribution and quantitative adelic extensions, plus the few known higher-dimensional cases.

proof_stages.label: Joint equidistribution theorem
proof_stages.summary: For products of pairwise non-isogenous algebraic groups into which a common sequence of tori is embedded, a fixed rank-at-least-two split part and individual near-equidistribution of each projection imply equidistribution of the orbit measures on the whole product, up to finite-index issues.

proof_stages.label: Joinings as obstructions to product limits
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines joinings as diagonal-invariant measures with prescribed marginals and explains that nontrivial joinings record relations between systems, while scarcity of self-joinings is the rigidity mechanism behind joint equidistribution.

proof_stages.label: Algebraic joining classification for higher-rank S-arithmetic actions
proof_stages.summary: For an S-arithmetic setting and a rank-two diagonalizable acting group built from two local multiplicative groups, every joining of the corresponding homogeneous actions is classified as Haar measure on a shifted orbit of an algebraic subgroup of the product.

proof_stages.label: Higher-rank necessity and completion of the argument
proof_stages.summary: The talk explains that rank-one diagonal actions have wild nonalgebraic joinings, whereas higher rank forces algebraicity; together with the absence of algebraic correspondences between non-isogenous factors and Ratner-type treatment of unipotent degenerations, this yields the joint equidistribution result.

examples.name: PGL2 centralizer torus and closed-geodesic packet
examples.note: A hyperbolic two-by-two integer matrix defines an anisotropic Q-torus in PGL2 whose adelic orbit projects to a finite packet of equal-length periodic diagonal orbits, making the general arithmetic-orbit definitions and Duke-type equidistribution concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: He concretely defines the PGL2 torus as the centralizer of a matrix gamma, chooses a shift diagonalizing gamma, projects the adelic orbit, and traces how it becomes a finite packet of periodic diagonal orbits/geodesics of equal length.

passes.reason: A centralizer torus in PGL2 giving a packet of closed geodesics is a standard arithmetic-orbit example, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.

examples.name: Anisotropic tori in PGL3
examples.note: The cited theorem for Q-anisotropic tori in PGL3, under a fixed local splitting hypothesis, illustrates how few higher-dimensional individual equidistribution results beyond the PGL2/Duke setting are presently known.
examples.engagement_reason: He states a known theorem for sequences of Q-anisotropic tori in PGL3 with a fixed split-place condition, but does not compute with or run a construction on a particular PGL3 torus.

passes.reason: Anisotropic tori in PGL3 form a systematic higher-rank family used as a positive equidistribution case, not a sporadic or limiting object.

examples.name: Integer points on spheres and shapes of orthogonal lattices
examples.note: For integer vectors of norm n, the talk pairs the normalized point on S^2 with the GL2(Z)-class of the lattice x-perp intersect Z^3, giving a down-to-earth number-theoretic application of individual and joint equidistribution.
examples.engagement_reason: He constructs the example from integer sphere points by taking each point’s orthogonal lattice, computing/identifying its volume as sqrt(n), attaching a quadratic form of discriminant 4n, and formulating the resulting joint equidistribution.

passes.reason: The round sphere/lattice construction is governed by the highly regular Euclidean quadratic form and is used as an application of joint equidistribution.
The lattice-point packet is built from the round sphere and its orthogonal quadratic form, a maximally symmetric setting for a positive joint-equidistribution application.
The example is built from the round sphere/quadratic form x^2+y^2+z^2, a maximally orthogonally symmetric setting, and is used as an application of joint equidistribution.
The integer points lie on the highly rotationally symmetric sphere and are used in a positive joint-equidistribution application.

examples.name: Product, graph, and relatively independent joinings
examples.note: The product joining, the graph joining from an isomorphism, and the relatively independent joining over a common factor illustrate how joinings encode relations between measure-preserving systems.
examples.engagement_reason: He explicitly constructs the standard joining examples: the product joining, the graph joining from an isomorphism, and the relatively independent joining via disintegration over a common factor.

passes.reason: Among joinings, the product joining is the trivial independent case while graph joinings are deterministic/maximally coupled examples, introduced to illustrate the notion.

examples.name: Unipotent-action analogy via Ratner theory
examples.note: The speaker compares the diagonalizable joining theorem with the additive/unipotent case, where analogous algebraicity is supplied by Ratner's measure-classification theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: He invokes unipotent dynamics and Ratner theory as an analogy/tool and states that the analogous unipotent version is covered by Ratner, without working through a concrete unipotent example.

passes.reason: Unipotent actions are standard nilpotent/limit cases of semisimple torus dynamics here invoked as a Ratner-theory analogy, not as a failure case.
Unipotent actions are presented as non-semisimple degenerations of torus dynamics and as a positive Ratner-theory analogue.
Unipotent actions have all eigenvalues equal to one and are described as degenerations of torus orbits, serving as an analogy via Ratner theory.
Unipotent actions are non-semisimple limiting degenerations of diagonal actions and are invoked as a Ratner-theory analogy/tool.
unipotent actions have collapsed all-one spectra and arise as parabolic boundary degenerations of torus actions, invoked as a positive Ratner analogy

examples.name: Rank-one diagonal flow on products of SL2 quotients
examples.note: The one-parameter diagonal action on products of SL2(R) quotients has Bernoulli factors and many wild joinings, showing why the higher-rank/two-place assumption is necessary for algebraic joining rigidity.
examples.engagement_reason: He uses the one-parameter diagonal action on products of SL2 quotients as a concrete counterexample, reasoning that it is Bernoulli, has many factors of arbitrary entropy, and hence many wild joinings, unlike the higher-rank case.

passes.reason: The one-parameter diagonal action is the minimum-rank case and is explicitly used to show the higher-rank joining classification fails in rank one.
A one-parameter rank-one diagonal action is the minimal-rank boundary case and is invoked to show algebraic joining rigidity is false.
The one-parameter diagonal flow is the minimal-rank boundary case and is used to show the joining classification is false without higher rank.
The rank-one diagonal flow is the minimal-rank boundary case and is used to show the higher-rank joining classification fails.
